title: Refining and presenting a programme
summary: This lesson provides a comprehensive guide to refining and presenting a musical programme for the IB Music SL 'Presenting' component. It focuses on interpreting the assessment criteria to build a coherent and diverse programme, and writing insightful, analytical programme notes that achieve top marks.
learningObjectives: Understand the assessment criteria for the Presenting component, particularly Criterion A (Coherence and diversity) and Criterion C (Programme notes).
Develop strategies for selecting and sequencing pieces to create a balanced, coherent, and diverse musical programme.
Learn to write concise, analytical, and well-justified programme notes that demonstrate musical understanding.
Analyse model programmes and notes to identify characteristics of high-scoring submissions.
Refine a personal programme and accompanying notes in preparation for the final performance assessment.

simpleExplanation.title: Curating Your Musical Exhibition
simpleExplanation.summary: Creating your performance programme is like being the curator of your own musical art exhibition. You don't just hang random paintings on a wall; you choose pieces that tell a story, show different styles, and highlight your unique artistic voice. The goal is to guide your audience (and the examiner) on a compelling journey.
simpleExplanation.analogy: Imagine you are a chef designing a three-course tasting menu. Your first course (your opening piece) should be engaging and set the tone. The main course (the core of your programme) should be substantial, showcasing your main skills and ideas. The dessert (your closing piece) should provide a satisfying conclusion. Each dish must be distinct (diversity), but they must also work together as a complete culinary experience (coherence). Your programme notes are the menu descriptions, explaining the ingredients (musical elements) and your cooking philosophy (interpretation).
steps: Brainstorm & Select: List potential pieces that showcase your technical and expressive skills. Select a shortlist that offers potential for both variety and connection.
Structure & Sequence: Arrange your selected pieces in a deliberate order. Consider pacing, key relationships, and the overall narrative or journey you want to create for the listener.

Justify & Annotate: For each piece, write programme notes that go beyond simple facts. Explain its musical features, its context, and your personal interpretive choices, justifying its place in the programme.

Refine & Rehearse: Practise the full programme in order. Assess the flow and transitions. Revise your piece selection or order if needed, and polish your programme notes based on your deepening understanding.

flashcards.back: The logical and musical connection between pieces in a programme. This can be achieved through shared themes, keys, historical progression, narrative, or a focus on a specific technical/expressive challenge.
The variety demonstrated within a programme. This should ideally encompass different historical periods, geographical origins, styles, tempi, moods, and technical/expressive demands.

The written text accompanying the programme. For IB Music, these must be analytical, justifying the choice of pieces and demonstrating understanding of their musical features, context, and interpretive decisions.
A key component of programme notes where you explain *why* a particular piece was included and how it contributes to the overall coherence and diversity of the programme.
Specific connections you identify between pieces, such as a shared chord progression, a similar melodic contour, a contrasting approach to rhythm, or a common formal structure.
Demonstrating awareness of the historical, social, and cultural background of the music, and explaining how this knowledge informs your interpretation.
The specific instrumental or vocal challenges presented by a piece (e.g., complex passagework, wide leaps, specific articulations, breath control).
The interpretative challenges of a piece, related to conveying mood, character, and emotion (e.g., shaping phrases, dynamic control, rubato, conveying a narrative).
The flow and timing of the overall programme. A well-paced programme balances energy levels, avoids monotony, and takes the listener on a satisfying journey.

In the context of programme notes, this means showing insightful, original, and nuanced understanding that goes beyond surface-level observations. It involves making sophisticated connections and detailed analytical points.
A top-band descriptor for Criterion A, indicating a programme that is well-suited to the performer's abilities while also demonstrating a commendable range of musical styles and challenges.
A top-band descriptor for Criterion A, indicating that the musical links and logic behind the programme's structure are immediately apparent and effectively realised.

faq.q: How many pieces should I have in my programme?
faq.a: There is no set number. The focus is on the total performance time (12-15 minutes for SL) and the quality of the programme's coherence and diversity. Most students present between 3 and 5 pieces, but this can vary depending on their length.

faq.q: Can all my pieces be by the same composer?
faq.a: While possible, it makes demonstrating diversity (Criterion A) very challenging. You would need to select pieces that show a significant range within the composer's output (e.g., an early vs. late work, or works in very different genres). It is generally safer and more effective to choose pieces from at least two different composers and historical periods.

faq.q: How much should I write for my programme notes?
faq.a: The absolute maximum is 500 words. Do not exceed this limit. Aim for concise, impactful writing. A well-structured 450-word text is far better than a rushed 500-word one. Focus on quality over quantity, ensuring every sentence serves a purpose: to analyse, justify, or explain an interpretive choice.

faq.q: What if I'm a vocalist? How can I show technical diversity?
faq.a: Vocalists can demonstrate technical diversity in many ways: performing in different languages, showcasing different parts of their vocal range (tessitura), using different vocal qualities (e.g., belting, head voice, falsetto), and tackling different stylistic demands (e.g., coloratura passages, sustained legato lines, rhythmic scat singing).

faq.q: My group piece is an improvisation. How do I write about it in the programme notes?
faq.a: This is a great opportunity. In your notes, you can't describe the exact improvisation, but you can and should describe the *framework* for it. Explain the underlying chord structure, the form, the intended style, and the roles each musician will play. Discuss the musical 'rules' or guidelines you've set for yourselves, and what you aim to explore through the improvisation. This demonstrates intentionality and musical understanding.

sections.content: Welcome to the final stage of your 'Presenting' journey: refining and presenting your programme. This is where your practical skills as a performer and your analytical skills as a musician converge. A successful programme is more than just a list of pieces you can play well; it is a curated, thoughtful musical statement that demonstrates the breadth and depth of your musicianship. This lesson will equip you to move beyond simply performing pieces to truly *presenting* a programme that is coherent, diverse, and intelligently justified.

To excel in this component, you must internalise the language of the markbands. The assessment is not just about how well you play (Criterion B), but also about the intelligence of your programming (Criterion A) and the quality of your written justification (Criterion C). Let's break them down.

items: **Criterion A: Coherence and diversity (6 marks)**: This criterion assesses the musical journey you design. 'Coherence' means the programme makes sense as a whole. 'Diversity' means it shows variety. A top-scoring programme is both 'clear and consistent' in its logic and 'appropriate and varied' in its content.

**Criterion C: Programme notes (6 marks)**: This criterion assesses your ability to communicate your musical understanding in writing. Examiners are looking for 'perceptive' and 'critically evaluative' comments. You must justify your choices, analyse musical features, and discuss context and interpretation. Simply describing the pieces is not enough.

sections.content: Coherence provides the underlying logic for your programme. It's the 'why' behind your selection and ordering. Think about creating a thread that connects your pieces. This thread can take many forms.

items: **Thematic/Narrative:** Do the pieces share a common story, mood, or extra-musical idea (e.g., 'Music of the Night', 'Sketches of Spain')?
**Historical Progression:** You could trace the evolution of a genre or an instrument, for example, moving from a Baroque sonata to a Classical sonata to a Romantic sonata.
**Focus on a Musical Element:** Your programme could explore different approaches to harmony, rhythm, or form. For example, a programme exploring 'Theme and Variations' across different eras.

**Juxtaposition and Contrast:** Coherence can also be created by deliberately placing highly contrasting pieces next to each other to highlight their differences. The coherence comes from the *purposeful* nature of the contrast.
**Key Relationships:** Structuring your programme around related keys (e.g., relative major/minor, dominant/tonic) can create a subtle but powerful sense of harmonic cohesion.

sections.content: Don't force a connection where one doesn't exist. The most convincing coherence is often simple and elegant. A programme showing the development of your own technical skills on your instrument can be a perfectly valid and coherent concept, as long as you articulate it clearly in your notes.

sections.question:
Analyse the following solo piano programme for its coherence and diversity. Suggest what the programme notes should highlight.

1. J.S. Bach - Prelude and Fugue in C minor, BWV 847 (from The Well-Tempered Clavier, Book I)
2. Frédéric Chopin - Nocturne in C-sharp minor, Op. posth.
3. Claude Debussy - 'La cathédrale engloutie' (from Préludes, Book I)
sections.solution:
This programme demonstrates excellent coherence and diversity, making it suitable for a high-scoring submission.

**Coherence:** The programme is coherent through its exploration of keyboard textures and harmonic language across three distinct periods. The central key of C minor/major acts as a subtle anchor: the Bach is in C minor, the Chopin begins and ends in C-sharp minor (enharmonically related and emotionally close), and the Debussy rises out of the depths to a powerful C major climax. The overarching theme could be described as 'From Structured Counterpoint to Impressionistic Colour'.

**Diversity:** The programme is highly diverse:
*   **Stylistically:** It moves from the structured counterpoint of the Baroque (Bach) to the lyrical melody and rich harmony of the Romantic era (Chopin), and finally to the atmospheric textures and non-functional harmony of Impressionism (Debussy).
*   **Technically:** It demands different skills: finger independence and clarity for the Bach fugue, delicate touch and cantabile playing for the Chopin, and sophisticated pedalling and dynamic control for the Debussy.
*   **Expressively:** It covers a wide emotional range, from the intellectual rigour of the fugue to the melancholic introspection of the nocturne and the majestic grandeur of the cathedral.

**Programme Notes Focus:** The notes should justify the programme's narrative arc. They must analyse specific musical features: the contrapuntal texture in the Bach, the use of rubato and ornamentation in the Chopin, and the use of planing, modality, and pedal in the Debussy to create atmosphere. The student should explain how the C minor/major tonal centre provides a subtle link, and how each piece presents a unique interpretive challenge that contributes to their development as a pianist.

sections.content: Your programme notes (max 500 words) are your chance to speak directly to the examiner and demonstrate the thinking behind your performance. They are not a history lesson or a biography of the composer. They are a justification and analysis. To score in the top band (5-6 marks for Criterion C), your notes must be 'critically evaluative' and 'perceptive'.

items: **Structure is Key:** Start with a brief overview (1-2 sentences) explaining the overall concept of your programme. Then, dedicate a paragraph to each piece.

**Go Beyond the Obvious:** Instead of saying 'This piece is in sonata form', analyse *how* the composer uses or subverts the form. Instead of saying 'The mood is sad', explain *how* the music creates that mood (e.g., 'through the use of a descending minor-key melody, slow tempo, and sparse accompaniment').

**Link to Performance:** Connect your analysis to your own interpretation. For example: 'To emphasise the improvisatory character of this cadenza-like passage, I will employ a degree of rubato, slightly delaying the resolution to build tension.'

**Justify Everything:** For each piece, explicitly state why it is in the programme. How does it contribute to the coherence? How does it add to the diversity? What specific challenge does it present for you as a performer?
**Use Specific Vocabulary:** Refer to melody, harmony, rhythm, texture, timbre, and form with precision. This demonstrates your musical literacy.

sections.question:
Below is an excerpt from a programme note for a group performance (vocalist, piano, bass) of the jazz standard 'Autumn Leaves'. Critique it and suggest improvements.

**Original:** 'We chose 'Autumn Leaves' because it is a famous jazz standard written by Joseph Kosma. It has an AABC form and is popular at jam sessions. It has a sad feeling, like autumn.'

**Task:** Rewrite this to meet the 'perceptive' and 'critically evaluative' markband descriptors.
sections.solution:
**Critique of Original:** The original note is purely descriptive and lacks depth. It states basic, easily found facts ('famous jazz standard', 'AABC form') without any analysis or justification. The comment on the 'sad feeling' is superficial. It would score in the 1-2 mark band for Criterion C.

**Improved, High-Scoring Version:**
'Our programme explores the evolution of harmonic interpretation in jazz, and Kosma’s ‘Autumn Leaves’ serves as our foundational text. We selected this standard specifically for its diatonic circle-of-fifths progression, which provides a clear harmonic canvas for improvisational exploration. Our arrangement begins with a sparse, rubato vocal and piano introduction to establish the song's melancholic lyricism, reflecting the 'autumnal' theme. The first chorus is played in a traditional swing style to honour the tune's history. However, for the solo sections, the piano will introduce chord substitutions, such as tritone substitutions on the dominant chords (e.g., using G♭7 instead of C7), to create more modern harmonic tension against the bass's walking line. This choice allows us to demonstrate a diversity of improvisational languages—from bebop-inflected lines to more contemporary modal colours—while remaining coherent through the song's underlying structure. This piece challenges us as a group to listen intently and react to these spontaneous harmonic alterations, showcasing our interactive musicianship.'

sections.content: For Music SL, your programme must include at least one piece performed as a soloist and at least one piece performed as part of a group. This is a mandatory requirement. Your programme notes must address this explicitly, explaining how the different performance formats contribute to the overall diversity of your presentation.

items: **Showcasing Different Skills:** Use your notes to explain what different skills are demonstrated in each format. Solo performance might highlight your individual technical command and interpretive authority. Group performance showcases skills like listening, interaction, blending, and responding to other musicians.

**Coherence Across Formats:** Find a way to link your solo and group pieces. For example, you could perform a solo piano arrangement of a song, and then perform the same song in a group setting, using your notes to compare the different interpretive possibilities.

**Role in the Ensemble:** When writing about your group piece, be specific about your role. Are you providing the harmonic foundation? Are you a melodic voice? Are you responsible for keeping time? How does your part interact with the others? This demonstrates a deeper understanding of ensemble playing.

sections.content: The balance between solo and group work is up to you, as long as the minimum requirement is met. A common and effective structure is to have 2-3 solo pieces and 1-2 group pieces, or vice versa, depending on your strengths and programme concept. Ensure your total performance time is between 12 and 15 minutes.
